notes: Arturia Solina V emulates the ARP Solina String Ensemble (also sold as the ARP Pro/DX and Eminent 310 Unique), the iconic 1970s string machine used by ABBA, Jean-Michel Jarre, Tangerine Dream, and countless pop and progressive rock artists. The Solina uses organ-style divide-down oscillators to produce all 49 notes simultaneously — there is no envelope, no filter, and no polyphony limit — with the output fed through a bucket-brigade device (BBD) ensemble chorus that gives the instrument its warm, shimmering, detuned character. Separate sections for Cellos, Violas, Violins, Brass, and Choir can be blended to taste. The ensemble/chorus effect is not decorative but integral to the sound — the Solina without chorus sounds thin and clinical.

Expression.description: Expression — scales overall output level for dynamics

Modulation.description: Modulation wheel — assignable modulation, often vibrato depth

Strings Level.description: String section level — combined level of Cellos, Violas, and Violins registers

Brass Level.description: Brass section level — bright, buzzy brass ensemble layer

Choir Level.description: Choir/vocal section level — adds a human voice-like layer to the ensemble

Ensemble Rate.description: Ensemble/BBD chorus rate — speed of the bucket-brigade ensemble effect; the defining Solina character control

Ensemble Depth.description: Ensemble depth — intensity of the BBD chorus modulation; deeper values give wider, more lush detuning

Attack.description: Amplitude envelope attack — the Solina has a slow attack option; longer attacks give bowed string phrasing

Release.description: Amplitude envelope release — tail length after keys are released

Reverb.description: Reverb send level — hall or plate reverb suits the orchestral character of the Solina

Tone.description: Tone/brightness — overall tonal shaping; roll off treble for lush warmth or open fully for vintage brightness
